skill.name: azure-appconfiguration-java
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure App Configuration SDK for Java to manage key-values, labels, feature flags, secret references, snapshots, and client setup.
skill.summary: Use Azure App Configuration from Java applications with SDK examples for settings, feature flags, labels, and snapshots.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found a single documentation file with markdown Java, XML, and bash examples. The backtick and blocker detections are false positives from fenced code blocks and Azure terminology; remaining concerns are low-risk examples that reference environment variables, Azure endpoints, and configuration values.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as External Commands
low_findings.description: The numerous external command alerts are false positives. The referenced lines are markdown fenced XML and Java examples, not executable Ruby or shell commands run by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is documentation, and the reported lines are fenced snippets for Maven and Java SDK use. No evidence found that the skill itself executes these snippets.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples for Azure Credentials
low_findings.description: The skill shows Java examples that read Azure App Configuration connection and endpoint variables. This is legitimate SDK setup guidance, but users should avoid exposing connection strings in prompts or logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: System.getenv is directly shown for Azure SDK configuration. The examples use expected Azure variables and do not send values to an unrelated destination.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Azure URLs and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The URL findings point to placeholder Azure service endpoints, a Key Vault secret URI example, and official reference links. They do not indicate hidden network calls by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation examples and references. No evidence found of code that automatically contacts these URLs from the skill.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Pattern Alerts Are Placeholder Documentation
low_findings.description: The database and secret-reference examples describe configuration keys and Azure Key Vault references. They contain placeholders or sample values, not real credentials or a credential directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are static documentation and do not include a real secret. The line flagged as an Azure credentials directory is an official Maven package link.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic algorithms. The cited areas discuss Azure App Configuration concepts and feature flag descriptions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or another weak cryptographic algorithm in these locations. The alerts appear triggered by unrelated words in documentation.

low_findings.title: Composite Critical Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code fences, Azure URLs, and environment variable examples into a critical heuristic. Manual review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential theft, or automated exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The component signals are present only as documentation examples and reference links. No executable file or instruction asks the agent to collect, encode, or transmit credentials.

content.user_title: Build Azure App Configuration Java Clients
content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable patterns for centralized configuration, feature flags, and release snapshots. This skill provides focused Azure SDK examples for clients, settings, labels, secret references, and snapshots.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for azure-data-appconfiguration.
Explains synchronous and asynchronous ConfigurationClient creation.
Covers create, read, update, delete, and conditional setting operations.
Demonstrates labels, key filters, revisions, and setting selectors.
Provides feature flag, secret reference, and snapshot examples.
Includes Azure documentation and sample links for follow-up research.
limitations: It is reference guidance, not a runnable Java project.
It does not validate live Azure resources or permissions.
Version examples may need review against current Azure SDK releases.
Users must supply their own secure credential and deployment practices.

use_cases.title: Add centralized configuration to a Java service
use_cases.description: Use the SDK examples to create a client, read labeled settings, and update application configuration safely.

use_cases.title: Plan feature flag rollout logic
use_cases.description: Use the feature flag examples to model percentage filters, enablement changes, and review output handling.

use_cases.title: Document Azure configuration operations
use_cases.description: Use the examples as source material for internal runbooks covering labels, snapshots, and secret references.

prompt_templates.title: Create a client
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a Java application.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create a Java Azure App Configuration client using Entra ID and explain each required value.

prompt_templates.title: Read settings by label
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me read Azure App Configuration settings with the Production label and handle missing settings in Java.

prompt_templates.title: Design feature flags
prompt_templates.scenario: Controlled rollout for a new capability.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Java feature flag flow using Azure App Configuration with percentage rollout and clear update steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build a release snapshot workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a release workflow for Azure App Configuration snapshots in Java, including creation, listing, archiving, and recovery checks.

output_examples.input: Ask for Java client setup using Entra ID.
output_examples.output: A concise setup guide with dependency notes, credential prerequisites, endpoint handling, and a client creation outline.

output_examples.input: Ask how to manage a production feature flag.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step explanation covering flag creation, percentage filters, state updates, and validation checks.
A release-oriented checklist explaining filters, snapshot creation, polling, listing, archiving, and recovery behavior.

best_practices: Prefer Entra ID authentication over connection strings when possible.
Use labels and snapshots to separate environments and preserve release state.
Avoid printing configuration values that may contain secrets or operational details.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real Azure connection strings into prompts or examples.
Do not store secret values directly when Key Vault references are appropriate.
Do not update production settings without ETag or read-only protection checks.

faq.question: What does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Java SDK usage for Azure App Configuration clients, settings, labels, feature flags, secret references, and snapshots.

faq.question: Does it run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation guidance and does not execute Azure CLI, Maven, Java, or network commands by itself.

faq.question: Can it help with feature flags?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for creating, reading, updating, and filtering feature flags with the Java SDK.

faq.question: Does it include real credentials?
faq.answer: No. The credential and endpoint values are placeholders that users must replace securely in their own environment.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production guidance?
faq.answer: It provides useful patterns, but teams should review current Azure SDK documentation and internal security policies before production use.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
